vulnerabilities.title: CauldronV4 accepts zero oracle exchange rates, enabling near-zero-collateral borrowing
vulnerabilities.summary: `init()` stores the oracle rate returned by `oracle.get(oracleData)` without validating success or non-zero value. `updateExchangeRate()` also accepts and stores any updated rate, including zero. `_isSolvent()` computes the debt-side requirement as `borrowPart * totalBorrow.elastic * exchangeRate / totalBorrow.base`; when `exchangeRate == 0`, this term becomes zero, so any position with minimal non-zero collateral passes solvency checks regardless of debt size.

description.desc: Oracle rates are accepted without validating `rate > 0`; solvency checks multiply debt by `exchangeRate`, so a zero rate collapses required collateral to zero.

vulnerabilities.impact: Protocol insolvency and direct loss of lendable MIM: borrowers can extract MIM against negligible collateral whenever a zero exchange rate is accepted.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. A Cauldron instance reaches `exchangeRate == 0` (for example during initialization or a later oracle update returning zero). 2. Attacker adds minimal collateral share to avoid the `collateralShare == 0` insolvency shortcut. 3. Attacker borrows MIM repeatedly up to available liquidity/borrow caps. 4. Post-borrow solvency checks still pass because debt valuation is multiplied by zero. 5. Attacker exits with undercollateralized borrowed MIM, leaving protocol bad debt.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Fail closed on invalid oracle outputs: require `updated && rate > 0` (or at minimum `rate > 0`) before persisting/using rates in `init()` and `updateExchangeRate()`. Revert borrow/removal flows when valid rates are unavailable.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLpAggregator misprices LP tokens when pool/base decimals are not 18
vulnerabilities.summary: `latestAnswer()` scales base/quote reserves to 1e18 units, then divides by raw LP total supply. In MagicLP, LP token decimals are `decimals() = IERC20Metadata(_BASE_TOKEN_).decimals()`, not fixed 18. For pools where base decimals are lower than 18 (e.g., 6), denominator units are 1e6 while numerator is 1e18-normalized, inflating the oracle answer by roughly `10^(18-baseDecimals)`.

description.desc: Reserves are normalized to 18 decimals, but `pair.totalSupply()` is used unnormalized even though MagicLP LP token decimals track the base token.

vulnerabilities.impact: Severe collateral overvaluation and over-borrowing, leading to protocol insolvency in markets relying on this oracle output.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. A market integrates this aggregator for a MagicLP pool whose base token has non-18 decimals. 2. Attacker acquires LP shares and supplies them as collateral in a Cauldron using this oracle path. 3. Oracle overvalues LP collateral due to denominator scale mismatch. 4. Attacker borrows more MIM than true collateral value and leaves bad debt.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Normalize LP supply to 18-decimal precision before division (or keep all terms in consistent native units). Add deployment-time checks to reject unsupported decimal domains.
